title: Arrays
summary: Learn how to store and work with ordered lists of data using JavaScript arrays, from basic indexing to the powerful map, filter, and reduce methods.

blocks.md: Imagine writing an app that tracks your favourite movies. You could create a separate variable for each one — `movie1`, `movie2`, `movie3` — but that gets messy fast. What if you have 50 movies? That is exactly the problem **arrays** solve. An array is a single variable that holds an **ordered list** of values, and JavaScript gives you a rich set of tools to read, update, and transform that list with very little code.

blocks.text: Creating an Array and Reading Its Length

blocks.caption: Square brackets create an array; .length tells you how many elements it has

blocks.title: Think of a row of lockers
blocks.md: Picture school lockers numbered starting from **0**, not 1. The first locker is locker 0, the second is locker 1, and so on. JavaScript arrays work exactly the same way — the first element lives at index `0`. This trips up almost every beginner at least once!

blocks.code:
const fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"];

console.log(fruits[0]); // first element
console.log(fruits[2]); // third (last) element
console.log(fruits[3]); // out of bounds — no error, just undefined
blocks.caption: Access elements with bracket notation. Last valid index = length - 1

blocks.title: Off-by-one errors are silent
blocks.md: Trying to read an index that does not exist returns `undefined` — JavaScript will NOT throw an error. That means bugs like `fruits[3]` on a 3-element array can be easy to miss. Always remember: **last valid index = array.length - 1**.

blocks.code:
const queue = ["Alice", "Bob"];

queue.push("Carol");           // add to END
console.log(queue);            // ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Carol']

queue.pop();                   // remove from END
queue.unshift("Zara");         // add to FRONT
queue.shift();                 // remove from FRONT
console.log(queue);            // ['Alice', 'Bob']

console.log(queue.includes("Alice")); // true
console.log(queue.includes("Zara"));  // false
blocks.caption: push/pop act on the end; unshift/shift act on the front; includes() checks membership

blocks.output:
Planet: Mercury
Planet: Venus
Planet: Earth
Planet: Mars
blocks.caption: for...of visits every element in order — clean and readable

blocks.text: The Big Three: map, filter, and reduce

blocks.caption: map transforms every element; filter keeps elements that pass a test; reduce boils the list to one value. They never mutate the original, and you can chain them: numbers.filter(n => n > 2).map(n => n * 10)

blocks.question: What does `[10, 20, 30][1]` evaluate to?
blocks.explanation: Arrays use zero-based indexing. Index 0 is 10, index 1 is 20, and index 2 is 30. So [10, 20, 30][1] gives you 20.

keyTakeaways: Arrays store ordered lists in a single variable using square brackets `[ ]`.
Indexing starts at 0 — the first element is always at index 0, and the last is at `length - 1`.
Use `push`/`pop` to add or remove from the end, and `unshift`/`shift` for the front.
`map`, `filter`, and `reduce` transform arrays without mutating the original — they are the backbone of modern JS data processing.
`includes()` is the quickest way to check whether a value is present in an array.

exercise.prompt: You have an array of product prices. Write code that: (1) adds a new price of 15 to the end of the array using push, (2) uses filter to keep only prices above 10, and (3) uses map to apply a 10% discount to those filtered prices. Log the final discounted prices.
exercise.hint: The filter callback should return `price > 10`. The map callback should return `price * 0.9`. Call push() before filtering so the new price is included.
